rs10156191.name: C>T

CC.interpretation: For c>t, your DAO variant is one contributing factor — standard histamine metabolism

CT.interpretation: For c>t, your DAO variant is one contributing factor — heterozygous variant reduces dao activity

TT.interpretation: Homozygous variant notably impairs histamine breakdown This is a statistical association that interacts with lifestyle, environment, and other genetic factors.

C/T.interpretation: Population studies link your DAO variant to altered probability for c>t. This reflects group-level statistics; individual risk depends substantially on lifestyle, family history, and other genes.
recommendations: Discuss c>t screening with a healthcare provider if family history is present
Lifestyle modifications often reduce genetic risk — diet, exercise, and avoiding known triggers matter

bibliography: Ayuso P., García-Martín E., et al. Genetic variability of human diamine oxidase: occurrence of three
nonsynonymous polymorphisms and study of their effect on serum enzyme activity. Pharmacogenet
Comas-Basté O., Sánchez-Pérez S., et al. Histamine Intolerance: The Current State of the Arts.
Biomolecules. 2020 Aug 14;10(8):1181.
García-Martín E., Ayuso P., et al. Histamine pharmacogenomics. Pharmacogenomics. 2009
García-Martín E., Martínez C.., et al. Diamine oxidase rs10156191 and rs2052129 variants are associated
with the risk for migraine. Headache. 2015 Feb;55(2):276-86.
rs10156191.technical_report:
Histamine needs to be metabolized in the body to prevent its accumulation and adverse
effects. To do this, histamine can follow two pathways: by means of the enzyme diamine
oxidase (DAO) or by means of histamine-N-methyltransferase (HNMT). The function of the DAO enzyme can be altered by several factors
Genetic variations. Binding of biogenic amines that can be present in certain foods (putrescine, cadaverine,
agamatine). They are usually present in fermented foods or foods that are altered by
microorganisms. Interaction with alcohol and its metabolites (acetaldehyde). Antibiotics such as clavulanic acid, cholestimate and cefuroxime, the antihypertensives
verapamil and dihydralazine, the analgesic metamizole, the antihistaminesThe
antidepressant amitriptyline, the muscle relaxant suxamethonium, ascorbic acid (vitamin
C) and thiamine (vitamin B1). The vast majority of studies that have analyzed the relationship between genetics and
histamine intolerance have focused mainly on the study of variations in the AOC1 gene,
which codes for the production of the enzyme DAO, which is the main pathway for histamine
degradation. From these studies it has been shown that individuals who are carriers of
variants in AOC1 produce a DAO enzyme with lower activity, which increases the likelihood
of accumulating histamine in the body. The most relevant variants in the AOC1 gene that affect the function of the DAO enzyme in
Caucasians are Thr16Met, Ser332Phe, and especially His645Asp. Another well-known
variant is G4586T, which is found in the promoter region of the AOC1 gene and has been
related to a decrease in its expression. Knowing the genetic predisposition to histamine intolerance can be helpful in determining
the origin of the intolerance. However, it alone does not explain why a person will eventually
develop histamine intolerance, so your results cannot be considered a diagnostic test. As
previously mentioned, other factors such as the presence of intestinal diseases, alcohol
consumption and drugs that inhibit DAO activity are known to play a role and if you have any
symptoms you should consult your physician for a proper evaluation.
rs10156191.description:
Histamine intolerance
Histaminosis or excess histamine in the blood occurs when there is a high intake of
histamine in our diet or when it is not metabolized and eliminated correctly.

recommendations: May have lower cardiovascular disease risk than average

TT.interpretation: e2/e2 - linked to lower cardiovascular risk but possible elevated type III hyperlipidemia This is a statistical association that interacts with lifestyle, environment, and other genetic factors.

TT.effect: Higher aromatase activity — elevated estrogen conversion from androgens
TT.interpretation: elevated conversion of androgens to estrogens — in men: potential for higher estradiol, lower free testosterone ratio; in women: higher estrogen exposure and estrogen-sensitive tissue effects This is a statistical association that interacts with lifestyle, environment, and other genetic factors.
recommendations: Men: Track testosterone/estradiol ratio; symptoms of high estrogen include water retention, mood changes, low libido
Men: cruciferous vegetables (DIM/I3C) help support estrogen clearance
Women: regular breast and gynecologic screening per guidelines
Both: limit alcohol (potent aromatase inducer) and excess body fat (primary aromatase tissue)

CC.interpretation: For c>t, your CYP19A1 variant is one contributing factor — baseline aromatase expression — standard androgen-to-estrogen conversion rate

GG.effect: Impaired IRS-1 signaling — higher insulin resistance risk
GG.interpretation: lower insulin receptor substrate-1 function — elevated type 2 diabetes risk (~1.5x) and higher fasting insulin; IRS-1 is the primary intracellular signal from the insulin receptor This is a statistical association that interacts with lifestyle, environment, and other genetic factors.
recommendations: Low-glycemic index diet Worth prioritizing (reduces IRS-1 demand)
Resistance training 3x/week — increases GLUT4 expression independent of IRS-1 pathway
Berberine 500mg twice daily (AMPK activator, IRS-1 independent insulin sensitizer)
Magnesium 400mg (required cofactor for insulin receptor kinase; often depleted in IR)
Continuous glucose Tracking if available — optimise postprandial spikes

AA.interpretation: For g>a, your IRS1 variant is one contributing factor — standard insulin receptor substrate signaling — no genetically elevated insulin resistance from this variant

TT.interpretation: For a148v, your PPARGC1B variant is one contributing factor — the tt genotype corresponds to the coding-strand ser/ser genotype
recommendations: Use an 8-12 week training block and objective performance measures before changing programming
Do not add supplements from this genotype alone

GG.scienceSimplified: Your cellular growth switch stays on too easily, reducing your cells' ability to clean out damaged components

rs1143634.technical_report:
The fact that a greater amount of muscle damage occurs due to the second phase of
exercise depends on the intensity of the inflammatory response carried out by the immune

 system. Three markers associated with the production of inflammatory activity compounds
produced by the immune system and located in the TNF, IL1B and IL6 genes have
been identified. One of the genes found is the TNF gene, responsible for the production of tumor necrosis
factor, an inflammatory activity factor associated with insulin levels and predisposition to
metabolic disorders, as well as activation of muscle protein degradation (via proteasome). This is why this factor is important in the processes of injury and remodeling of
muscle tissue. The other two markers are found in the IL1B gene, which produces interleukin 1B, whose
expression in muscle fibers is elevated a few days after physical exercise, also inducing the
expression of other proinflammatory genes; and in the IL6 gene, which produces interleukin
6, with proinflammatory activity. research indicates that, after eccentric exercise, muscle
can induce local expression of the cytokine IL-6.
rs1143634.description:
PDF

Exercise-induced muscle damage (second phase)
In the second phase of exercise-induced muscle damage, an inflammatory process takes
place in which certain genetic variants may contribute to more or less muscle damage.

TT.effect: Reduced 7-DHC availability — lower vitamin D3 synthesis from sunlight
TT.interpretation: DHCR7 TT variant diverts 7-dehydrocholesterol away from vitamin D3 synthesis pathway; notably lower sun-exposed vitamin D production; linked to vitamin D deficiency even with adequate sun exposure This is a statistical association that interacts with lifestyle, environment, and other genetic factors.
recommendations: Vitamin D3 3000-5000IU daily (substantially higher than standard; sun exposure unreliable)
Test 25-OH vitamin D quarterly until stable in 50-70 ng/mL range
Vitamin K2 MK-7 100mcg with vitamin D (directs calcium to bone, not arteries)
Magnesium 400mg (cofactor for vitamin D metabolism at multiple steps)
Fatty fish 3x/week provides pre-formed vitamin D3 not dependent on this pathway

GT.interpretation: One TT risk allele — moderately lower vitamin D synthesis capacity This is a statistical association that interacts with lifestyle, environment, and other genetic factors.
recommendations: Vitamin D3 2000-3000IU and regular 25-OH vitamin D testing

GG.effect: Normal DHCR7 — efficient vitamin D3 synthesis
GG.interpretation: For g>t, your DHCR7 variant is one contributing factor — standard 7-dehydrocholesterol availability for vitamin d synthesis — normal sun-to-vitamin-d conversion
recommendations: 20-30 min daily sun exposure to midday skin; supplement 1000-2000IU if sun-limited

TT.effect: Reduced thyroid peroxidase activity — lower thyroid hormone synthesis
TT.interpretation: lower TPO enzyme activity — may require more iodine and selenium support for adequate thyroid hormone synthesis; linked to subclinical hypothyroidism susceptibility and elevated TSH This is a statistical association that interacts with lifestyle, environment, and other genetic factors.
recommendations: Ensure adequate iodine intake: 150mcg/day (seaweed, iodized salt, seafood)
Selenium 100-200mcg (required cofactor for TPO function and thyroid hormone synthesis)
Annual thyroid panel: TSH, free T3, free T4, anti-TPO antibodies
Avoid goitrogens in excess (raw cruciferous vegetables, soy in excess) without adequate iodine

TC.interpretation: One lower-activity allele — mild thyroid hormone synthesis vulnerability
recommendations: Adequate iodine and selenium intake; annual TSH check

CC.interpretation: For c>t, your TPO variant is one contributing factor — standard thyroid peroxidase function — normal thyroid hormone synthesis
recommendations: Standard iodine intake; standard thyroid Tracking per age guidelines

TT.interpretation: Lower SIRT6 chromatin remodeling/DNA repair activity — lower histone H3K9 deacetylation at telomeres, higher genomic instability, and reduced regulation of glucose metabolism genes This is a statistical association that interacts with lifestyle, environment, and other genetic factors.
recommendations: Minimize DNA-damaging exposures (UV, cigarette smoke, ionizing radiation)
Zinc 15mg (SIRT6 binds zinc ions for structural integrity)
Caloric restriction mimetics — SIRT6 is a key mediator of CR benefits
Resveratrol and quercetin maintain SIRT6 activity

TC.interpretation: One lower-activity allele — partial SIRT6 DNA repair and metabolic regulation impairment

CC.interpretation: For c>t, your SIRT6 variant is one contributing factor — wild-type sirt6 — standard telomere maintenance, dna repair, and metabolic gene regulation
recommendations: Caloric restriction and fasting maintain optimal SIRT6 activity

AA.effect: Impaired CDK5 regulatory subunit-associated protein — reduced beta-cell function
AA.interpretation: CDKAL1 variant impairs tRNA modification needed for accurate insulin biosynthesis; lower beta-cell capacity under glucose challenge; linked to ~1.5x higher type 2 diabetes risk This is a statistical association that interacts with lifestyle, environment, and other genetic factors.
recommendations: Low-glycemic diet to reduce beta-cell demand (less insulin secretion required)
Exercise 150+ min/week — CDKAL1 T2D risk attenuated by physical activity
Maintain BMI < 25 — excess weight amplifies beta-cell demand against CDKAL1 impairment
Annual HbA1c and 2-hour glucose tolerance test from age 35

AG.interpretation: One risk allele — mildly lower insulin biosynthesis accuracy under glucose load

GG.interpretation: For g>a, your CDKAL1 variant is one contributing factor — standard beta-cell trna modification and insulin synthesis accuracy

AA.effect: Reduced BHMT activity — impaired choline-dependent homocysteine remethylation
AA.interpretation: Lower betaine-homocysteine methyltransferase activity — lower liver homocysteine recycling via betaine pathway; elevated homocysteine especially on low-choline diets; the betaine pathway is the backup route when MTHFR is compromised This is a statistical association that interacts with lifestyle, environment, and other genetic factors.
recommendations: Betaine (trimethylglycine) 500-1000mg daily — directly provides BHMT substrate
Especially important if also carrying MTHFR C677T — dual methylation pathway support needed

AG.interpretation: One lower-activity allele — partial betaine methylation capacity
recommendations: Betaine supplementation and choline-rich foods beneficial

GG.interpretation: For g>a, your BHMT variant is one contributing factor — standard betaine-dependent homocysteine remethylation

rs28497577.name: Exercise-induced muscle damage (initial phase)

G/G.interpretation: Your MYLK2 genotype is the most common form in reference populations. This does not rule out exercise-induced muscle damage (initial phase) risk from other genes, environmental exposures, or rare variants not covered by standard genotyping.
recommendations: No specific MYLK2-directed intervention indicated, but standard health screening still applies
Other genetic and environmental factors contribute to exercise-induced muscle damage (initial phase) — genotype alone is not the full picture

bibliography: Baumert P, Lake MJ, Stewart CE, Drust B, Erskine RM. Genetic variation and exercise-induced
muscle damage: implications for athletic performance, injury and ageing. Eur J Appl Physiol, 2016;
Ben-Zaken S, Eliakim A, Nemet D, Rabinovich M, Kassem E, Meckel Y. ACTN3 Polymorphism: Comparison
Between Elite Swimmers and Runners. Sport Med, 2015;1(1):13.
Deuster PA, Contreras-Sesvold CL, OConnor FG, Campbell WW, Kenney K, Capacchione JF, et al. Genetic
polymorphisms associated with exertional rhabdomyolysis. Eur J Appl Physiol, 2013; 113(8):19972004.
Pimenta EM, Coelho DB, Cruz IR, Morandi RF, Veneroso CE, de Azambuja Pussieldi G, et al. The
ACTN3 genotype in soccer players in response to acute eccentric training. Eur J Appl Physiol, 2012;
rs28497577.technical_report:
There is evidence to indicate that early phase muscle damage, produced by mechanical
damage, prior to inflammation, can be associated with several genetic variants. In the first
phase or stage of muscle damage, alterations occur in the muscle structures responsible
for the contraction of the myofibrils, which are the contractile structures found inside the
muscle cells. Damage occurs in the proteins that form the myofibrils (such as collagen,

 actin, myosin, desmin, titin) and other proteins that interact with the cell cytoskeleton and
the extracellular matrix. Two polymorphisms associated with muscle damage produced in this first phase after
exercise have been identified. These markers are found in the ACTN3 and MYLK2 genes. The ACTN3 gene codes for a muscle-tendon interaction protein, and the marker analyzed
has implications for the risk of muscle injury. Individuals carrying two copies of this
mutation are unable to produce the alpha-actinin-3 protein and this hinders recovery. The
absence of alpha-actinin-3 does not result in disease due to the compensatory effect of the
alpha-actinin-2 protein, but it does affect fitness. The MYLK2 gene, on the other hand, mediates producing the enzyme "myosin light
chain kinase 2" which is expressed in adult skeletal muscle, in muscle fibers, and variants
affecting its production can be related to muscle strength and blood levels of creatine
kinase, a marker of muscle degradation. Both markers, in turn, have been linked to increased risk of rhabdomyolysis (breakdown of
muscle tissue that releases the contents of muscle fibers into the blood).
rs28497577.description:
PDF

Exercise-induced muscle damage (initial phase)
The presence of certain genetic variants could explain why some people are less likely to be
injured than others, as well as having an easier time recovering from prolonged and
strenuous exercise. Knowing how someone will respond to a particular type of exercise can
guide us in personalizing our workouts, while reducing the risk of injury associated with
muscle overload.

recommendations: Discuss exercise-induced muscle damage (initial phase) screening with a healthcare provider if family history is present
Lifestyle modifications often reduce genetic risk — diet, exercise, and avoiding known triggers matter

CC.interpretation: For exercise-induced muscle damage (initial phase), your ACTN3 variant is one contributing factor — the cc (r/r) genotype retains alpha-actinin-3 expression
recommendations: Choose training from measured sprint, jump, strength, and endurance response
Treat this as interesting context rather than a talent result

recommendations: Use repeatable performance tests and recovery data to guide training

TT.interpretation: The TT (X/X) genotype is linked to alpha-actinin-3 deficiency, a common non-disease state
recommendations: Train for your goals and compare actual power and endurance benchmarks
Do not select a sport or supplement from this genotype

rs13266634.technical_report:
As we have indicated, in order for muscle regeneration to occur after exercise, there must be
an inflammatory signal through which the satellite cells are activated. There are studies that

 relate certain genetic variants with this inflammatory signal and, therefore, with a greater or
lesser capacity for muscle remodeling. A lower capacity for muscle remodeling implies that
a longer recovery is required after an injury. Two genetic polymorphisms have been
identified that are related to this inflammatory signal and therefore to a greater or lesser
capacity for muscle regeneration. These polymorphisms are found in the SOD2 and
SLC30A8 genes. The SOD2 gene produces the superoxide dismutase 2 protein, which has antioxidant activity,
protecting the muscle from free radicals and facilitating muscle regeneration. The lack of
functionality of this protein can cause the accumulation of free radicals and cell death of
muscle fibers. On the other hand, the SLC30A8 gene is associated with insulin response, and is indirectly
associated with muscle strength and mass, as glycogen synthesis and glucose transport to
the muscle are affected.
rs13266634.description:
PDF

Exercise-induced muscle damage (regeneration capacity)
Variants affecting genes involved in the activation of the cells responsible for muscle
regeneration are relevant to understanding why some people require more time to recover
after a muscle injury.

rs333.display: CCR5 - CCR5Delta32 and susceptibility to HIV infection

TACAGTCAGTATCAATTCTGGAAGAATTTCCAG/TACAGT.interpretation: Your CCR5 genotype is the most common form in reference populations. This does not rule out ccr5delta32 and susceptibility to hiv infection risk from other genes, environmental exposures, or rare variants not covered by standard genotyping.

Other genetic and environmental factors contribute to ccr5delta32 and susceptibility to hiv infection — genotype alone is not the full picture

bibliography: Jasinska AJ et al CCR5 as a Coreceptor for Human Immunodeficiency Virus and Simian Immunodeficiency
Viruses: A Prototypic Love-Hate Affair. Front Immunol. 2022;13:835994.
Ellwanger JH et al. Beyond HIV infection: Neglected and varied impacts of CCR5 and CCR5Delta32 on viral
diseases. Virus Res. 2020;286:198040.
Scheller SH et al. Biallelic, Selectable, Knock-in Targeting of CCR5 via CRISPR-Cas9 Mediated Homology
Directed Repair Inhibits HIV-1 Replication. Front Immunol. 2022;13:821190.
Matti C et al. CCR5 deficiency/CCR5?32: resistant to HIV infection at the cost of curtailed CD4 + T cell
memory responses. EMBO J. 2020 Aug 3;39(15):e105854.
rs333.technical_report:
The presence in humans of the natural variant CCR5Delta32 consists of a 32 base pair
deletion of the DNA sequence, which leads to loss of function of the CCR5 gene, a coreceptor required for HIV access to T lymphocytes, the main target of this virus. The
presence of this variant modulates the risk of HIV transmission and counteracts the
pathogenesis of HIV infection, to a greater extent in homozygosis (two copies) and to a
lesser extent in heterozygosis (single copy).
rs333.description:
PDF

CCR5Delta32 and susceptibility to HIV infection
CCR5 acts as an important co-receptor used by HIV for cellular entry into TCD4+
lymphocytes. The presence of a natural 32 base pair deletion in the CCR5 gene, known as
CCR5Delta32, produces a truncated receptor that is not expressed on the surface of
lymphocytes, thus preventing viral access and conferring resistance to infection.

rs2814778.name: Duffy Antigen, malaria resistant

T/T.interpretation: Your DARC genotype is one of several genetic factors linked to duffy antigen, malaria resistant. Research in this area continues to evolve, and most traits have multiple genetic and environmental contributors.
recommendations: Stay informed as new research clarifies the role of DARC in duffy antigen, malaria resistant
Consider discussing with a healthcare provider familiar with genetic interpretation

bibliography: World Health Organizatio (WHO) Paludism. [May 2022]
Aldarweesh F. The Duffy Blood Group System. Human Blood Group Systems and Haemoglobinopathies.
London: IntechOpen; 2019 [May 2022].
Kaur H et al. Duffy antigen receptor for chemokines (DARC) and susceptibility to Plasmodium vivax
malaria. Parasitol Int. 2019 Aug;71:73-75.
Kano FS et al. Susceptibility to Plasmodium vivax malaria associated with DARC (Duffy antigen)
polymorphisms is influenced by the time of exposure to malaria. Sci Rep. 2018 Sep 14;8(1):13851.
Reich D et al. Reduced Neutrophil Count in People of African Descent Is Due To a Regulatory Variant in the
Duffy Antigen Receptor for Chemokines Gene. PLoS Genet. 2009 Jan; 5(1): e1000360.
rs2814778.technical_report:
The presence or absence of the Duffy antigen in erythrocytes is highly heritable, although it
is substantially more frequent in populations of African origin than in those of European
origin. Through a study of 6000 individuals of African-American ancestry, a marker has been
identified in the DARC or ACKR1 gene, responsible for the presence of this antigen in red
blood cells or erythrocytes and resistance to malaria.
rs2814778.description:
Duffy Antigen, malaria resistant
The Duffy antigen consists of a glycoprotein on the surface of erythrocytes that acts as a
key mediator for the access of the Plasmodium vivax parasite to the interior of red blood
cells during its life cycle. Thus, individuals who do not carry this antigen would be resistant
to the forms of malaria caused by this species.

rs4349859.name: HLA-B27 antigen

G/G.interpretation: Your MICA-AS1 genotype is the most common form in reference populations. This does not rule out hla-b27 antigen risk from other genes, environmental exposures, or rare variants not covered by standard genotyping.
recommendations: No specific MICA-AS1-directed intervention indicated, but standard health screening still applies
Other genetic and environmental factors contribute to hla-b27 antigen — genotype alone is not the full picture

bibliography: Bowness P. HLA-B27. Annu Rev Immunol. 2015;33:29-48.
Evans DM, et al. Interaction between ERAP1 and HLA-B27 in ankylosing spondylitis implicates peptide
handling in the mechanism for HLA-B27 in disease susceptibility. Nat Genet. 2011;43(8):761-7.
Jang HS, Proos A, Koe L, Anderson J, Fulton R, Fernando SL. High accuracy of HLA-B*27 genotyping by
allele-specific real-time polymerase chain reaction in a heterogeneous population compared to flow
cytometry and single nucleotide polymorphism detection assays. Pathology. 2020;52(2):256-261.
rs4349859.technical_report:
There is sufficient evidence for the rs4349859 single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP)
genotype to be considered a genetic marker of HLA-B27-positive individuals of Caucasian
ancestry, although it is not correlated in African or Asian populations. The presence of this marker is strongly associated with the development of rheumatic-type
diseases such as ankylosing spondylitis or rheumatoid arthritis. Only 4% of the European population has one copy of the B27 allele (genotype rs4349859
AG), according to the Ensembl database. This allele has been found to be highly prevalent in
males with AS. HLA-B27 is present in 8% of the healthy Caucasian population and in 90% of
AS patients. However, only 1-5% of HLA-B27-positive individuals develop AS, suggesting that
other genetic factors are involved.
rs4349859.description:
HLA-B27 antigen
Human leukocyte antigens (HLA) are proteins that help the body's immune system
differentiate between its own cells and foreign, harmful substances. The HLA-B27 protein,
located on the surface of white blood cells, has been linked to the development of
spondyloarthropathies, a group of inflammatory diseases including ankylosing spondylitis.

TT.interpretation: KCNQ1 T allele — the most replicated type 2 diabetes GWAS locus in East Asian populations; linked to beta-cell function impairment and lower insulin secretion; T2D is a major accelerator of biological aging This is a statistical association that interacts with lifestyle, environment, and other genetic factors.
recommendations: Low-glycemic diet strictly (carbohydrate quality paramount for KCNQ1 risk genotype)
Resistance training 3x/week (increases beta-cell function independent of KCNQ1)
Regular HbA1c Tracking (quarterly if impaired fasting glucose)
Berberine 500mg twice daily — improves beta-cell function

CT.effect: One risk allele — moderate T2D risk elevation

CC.interpretation: For c>t, your KCNQ1 variant is one contributing factor — standard kcnq1-mediated potassium channel function — standard insulin secretion

TT.effect: Arg262/Arg262 — pro-longevity but elevated autoimmune risk
TT.interpretation: SH2B3/LNK Arg262 variant — pleiotropic effects; positive selection driven by protection against infection in historical context; linked to longer lifespan in large cohort studies but also modestly elevated autoimmune risk
recommendations: Anti-inflammatory diet to balance the autoimmune tendency
Regular CRP and inflammatory marker Tracking
The longevity benefit appears to outweigh autoimmune risk in most environmental contexts

Carriers of multiple FOXO3 longevity variants have compounding benefit — maintain the lifestyle

Annual serum IGF-1 Tracking — target 120-200 ng/mL for balance of longevity and muscle maintenance
Resistance training to maintain lean mass without relying on IGF-1 elevation
